titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Report on Home Opinion - August 21, 1942

div.note: The period under review (from 17th July to date) has been marked by continuous and increasing disquiet about the Russian position. The bad news has put all other news into the background and, although sympathy and admiration for the Russian people remain high, the possibilities of a Russian collapse are now very much in people's minds.

This sense of disquiet seems to have had several consequences. It maintains, of course, the desire for a Second Front in Europe. I do not think that there is an agitation for this or that there is any instant public demand for it to be achieved. The hard realities have sunk in. But people are restless and unhappy before the spectacle of German advances in Russia, while we appear powerless to intervene: and it is rather that they long to be told that the time has come when the Second Front will be opened than that they urgently demand its opening.

Another sign is refusal to take a mental interest in the war. This is not at all inconsistent with a person being actively engaged in war work: but there is ample evidence of a fairly widespread decay of interest in war news, reduction of listening to the B.B.C. and avowed inability to follow what is going on. No doubt people are longing for good news: and when they do not get it they tend to dissociate their minds from news altogether.

Professions of readiness for further sacrifices are still very common. This feeling probably contributes to the high spirit with which German air raids are met by the people in the areas affected and, though it is widely expected that the weight of German air attacks will be intensified this winter they do not appear to be anticipated with alarm or foreboding. (This confidence is in part from a belief that we are now much better prepared for active and passive defence than we were in previous years.) Even the regular expressions of approval with which food rationing and distribution are received by the public seem a little embarrassed, and it is possible to detect a feeling that further restrictions would be accepted almost with relief.

"Holidays at home" have been a constant topic. The subject seems to have been taken and fixed upon by the public as a typical example of Government muddle. The most reasonable explanations as to the Government's share in the matter and as to the impossibility of any detailed system of rationing travel failed, at any rate in the earlier weeks, to mitigate the public irritation. From this I make two deductions that may be useful to record: (a) In its present mood the public attributes to Government action or inaction all the main incidents that affect its daily life and does not seriously attend to distinctions between various forms of responsibility. If things seem to go right, the Government is praised: if they seem to go wrong the Government is thought to be run by incompetent people. In other words the -public generalizes from what it seems. (b) If appeals are used and things still appear to be wrong, there is a regular hostile reaction on the lines "Why can't they say what they want, and then order everyone to do it instead of appealing to people to do things?"

The War Cabinet is aware of the special problem in international relations which is created by the arrival of large scale American forces in this country. It is early to speak, but I am glad to say that on the whole, reports of the British reaction to their visitors are genuinely favourable. Reports agree as to the fine type of man represented in the American forces. I hope that the Americans and the public will not be made self-conscious by too many people diagnosing and prescribing. The main "headache" is the very great disparity in the pay of the respective forces, which reacts both on the British soldiers and on the civilian population.

There seems to be practically no sign of disunity in the public reaction to events in India. There is solid support for the action of the authorities in arresting Gandhi and his followers and in taking firm action to repress rioting and sabotage. It may be that this is in part the usual favourable response to firm and decisive measures, and that more divergent voices will be heard if the civil disobedience movement does not quickly subside. But for the time being public feeling seems to be all one way.
